preamble: Affrication of alveolar fricative [s] to affricate [ts] represents a fortition process commonly triggered by word-initial position or post-nasal strengthening. This shift is well-documented in Old Chinese historical phonology, where fricatives strengthened to affricates before high vowels, contributing to the modern Mandarin sibilant inventory.

examples.note: Fricative strengthening to affricate in word-initial position before high vowels [i], creating the modern Mandarin ts (z) and tsh (c) distinction
Affrication of *s- before high vowel, leading to modern Mandarin cūn /tsʰwən/
Modern Mandarin shows optional strengthening of word-initial or post-nasal [s] to [ts] in rapid speech

sources: Norman, J. (1988). Chinese. Cambridge University Press.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Oxford University Press.
Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction.
